alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion about moderate performance owing to limited data, but the published statement expresses it with slightly lower certainty (medium vs high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only a few features, notably the distance from the rifampicin binding site, were found to be predictive on their own.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Measuring distances from molecular dynamics simulations did not improve the performance of the models.
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasized binding-site distance as predictive; the publication reports that MD-derived distance measures did not improve model performance, which revises/qualifies the earlier feature-specific claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All four machine learning models exhibited similar sensitivities and specificities in the range of 0.84-0.88 and 0.94-0.97.
alignments.rationale: The specific numeric performance comparison across four models present in the preprint does not appear in the publication text or is replaced by alternative model-preference statements, so the explicit claim was removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of machine learning with mechanistic modeling methods can develop computational models that more fully represent cell-line-specific drug responses.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed MEMMAL framework combines machine learning/statistical models with mechanistic models to better represent cellular behavior.
alignments.rationale: The publication formalizes the MEMMAL framework but preserves the preprint's core contribution that combining ML/statistical and mechanistic models improves representation of cellular behavior/drug responses.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning/statistical models built using omics datasets provide high confidence predictions for new interactions between genes and proteins where there is physicochemical uncertainty.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning/statistical models built using omics datasets can provide predictions for new interactions between genes and proteins where there is physicochemical uncertainty.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the preprint's claim about ML/statistical models providing predictions for novel interactions under physicochemical uncertainty; wording and intent are consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed framework allows for the incorporation of novel IFNγ/PD-L1 related associations into the SPARCED model to describe the cellular response to checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating novel IFNγ/PD-L1 related associations into a mechanistic model improves the recapitulation of the NIH LINCS Consortium MCF10A dataset.
alignments.rationale: The preprint framed incorporation as enabling description of immunotherapy responses, whereas the publication reports a more concrete dataset-recapitulation improvement; this is a narrower, more data-grounded claim and thus a revision/tempering of the original interpretation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work serves as a template for combining big data and machine-learning-inferred interactions with mechanistic models for broader applications in precision medicine.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integration of big-data-inferred interactions with mechanistic models could be broadly applicable for building multi-scale precision medicine and whole cell models.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the preprint's conclusion about broad applicability of integrating big-data-inferred interactions with mechanistic models for precision-medicine-scale modeling.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning was shown to be effective at identifying distinctive genomic signatures among viral sequences.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning was shown to be effective at identifying distinctive genomic signatures among viral sequences.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the preprint's result that ML effectively identifies distinctive viral genomic signatures verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: KEVOLVE was more effective at identifying variant-discriminative signatures than several gold-standard statistical tools.
alignments.pub_claim_text: KEVOLVE was more effective at identifying variant-discriminative signatures than several gold-standard statistical tools.
alignments.rationale: The claim about KEVOLVE outperforming other tools is preserved identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The majority of identified signatures were associated with known mutations among the different variants, in terms of functional and pathological impact based on available literature.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The majority of identified signatures were associated with known mutations among the different variants, in terms of functional and pathological impact based on available literature.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the preprint's observation linking identified signatures to known variant mutations and their reported impacts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: KEVOLVE is a robust machine learning approach to identify discriminative signatures among SARS-CoV-2 variants.
alignments.pub_claim_text: KEVOLVE is a robust machine learning approach to identify discriminative signatures among SARS-CoV-2 variants, which are frequently also biologically relevant.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds that many identified signatures are biologically relevant, strengthening the claim's implication of biological validity; this is a clarification with amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant phylogenetic signal in genetic diversity was found within amphibians. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Significant phylogenetic signal in genetic diversity was found within amphibians. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result verbatim, so the claim is unchanged in meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Salamander species at northern latitudes contain lower genetic diversity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Salamander species at northern latitudes contain low genetic diversity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same finding with only minor wording change ("lower" vs "low"); meaning and scope are the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Data repurposing and machine learning provide valuable tools for detecting patterns relevant for conservation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Data repurposing and machine learning are valuable tools for understanding global biodiversity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the core point about the value of data repurposing and ML but broadens the application from conservation pattern detection to understanding global biodiversity, clarifying and enlarging scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Life history traits were not important predictors of genetic diversity for this dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Life history traits were not important predictors of genetic diversity in the analyzed data set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same dataset-specific negative result with equivalent wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Concerted efforts are needed to compile meaningful datasets for understanding global biodiversity. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The published claim-list does not include an explicit call for concerted data compilation; this limitation/need is not represented in the publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning frameworks can effectively analyze genetic data to address questions in ecology and evolution. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit, broader statement about ML frameworks' effectiveness for ecological/evolutionary questions that was not present as a distinct claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The augmentation method produces a noticeable increase in QuipuNet's accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The augmentation method produces a noticeable increase in QuipuNet's accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim retained in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: YupanaNet achieves greater accuracy (95.8%) than QuipuNet (94.6%) on the same dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: YupanaNet achieves greater accuracy (95.8%) than QuipuNet (94.6%) on the same dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact numeric performance comparison is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: YupanaNet benefits from enhanced generalization provided by Brownian motion data augmentation and the incorporation of novel architectures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: YupanaNet benefits from enhanced generalization provided by Brownian motion data augmentation and the incorporation of novel architectures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The explanatory claim about the sources of YupanaNet's benefit is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Feedforward neural network (FFNN) provided with appropriate configuration is a promising technique to retrieve crop traits from input features consisting of 1 nm-wide hyperspectral bands across 400-2500 nm range and observation configuration, leading to a precise joint estimation for LAI, Cab, Cm, and Cw. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed conceptual framework has the potential to simultaneously retrieve multiple crop traits from canopy reflectance for applications in precision agriculture and plant breeding. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint makes a specific, high-confidence result about an FFNN and wavelength/observation configuration; the publication abstracts this into a more conceptual, potential framework claim with lower asserted certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A narrower range in 400-1100 nm without observation configuration in input of FFNN model provided less precise estimation for LAI, Cab, Cm, and Cw. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive model trained over a synthetic dataset with biological constraints enabled the prediction of leaf water content using wavelengths in the visible to near infrared range. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint reports a comparative negative result for multiple traits with a narrower wavelength range; the publication emphasizes a positive result for predicting leaf water content specifically using VNIR, shifting and narrowing the focus and tone.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The introduction of biological constraints in training datasets improved FFNN model performance in both average precision and stability, resulting in a much accurate estimation for LAI, Cab, Cm, and Cw. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The theoretical analysis demonstrated potential advantages of adding biological constraints in synthetic training datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core idea (value of biological constraints) is preserved but the publication frames it as theoretical potential rather than the stronger empirical-improvement claim in the preprint, reducing assertiveness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The GPU implementation can train networks of one million neurons, with 100 million plastic synapses and a billion static synapses, about 1000 times faster than an unoptimized reference CPU implementation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GPU implementation can train networks of one million neurons, with 100 million plastic synapses and a billion static synapses, about 1,000 times faster than an unoptimized reference CPU implementation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the quantitative performance claim with identical meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The code can train a network to reproduce the activity of over 66,000 recorded neurons of a mouse performing a decision-making task in less than an hour. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The code can train a network to reproduce the activity of over 66,000 recorded neurons of a mouse performing a decision-making task in less than an hour. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical empirical claim reproduced in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The fast implementation enables a more interactive in-silico study of the dynamics and connectivity underlying multi-area computations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The fast implementation enables a more interactive in-silico study of the dynamics and connectivity underlying multi-area computations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conceptual conclusion is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The implementation admits the possibility to train models as in-vivo experiments are being conducted, thus closing the loop between modeling and experiments. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The implementation allows for the possibility to train models while in-vivo experiments are being conducted, thus closing the loop between modeling and experiments. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Wording is slightly altered but the claim about closing the loop between modeling and experiments is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neural networks with high-dimensional image subspaces tended to have better generalization performance when predicting cortical responses to held-out stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neural networks with high-dimensional image subspaces tended to have better generalization performance when predicting cortical responses to held-out stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same empirical finding with equivalent wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High dimensionality was associated with better performance when learning new categories of stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: High dimensionality was associated with better performance when learning new categories of stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about performance on new stimulus categories is reproduced unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher dimensional representations are better suited to generalize beyond their training domains. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher dimensional representations are better suited to generalize beyond their training domains. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conceptual conclusion preserved exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High-dimensional geometry confers computational benefits to DNN models of visual cortex. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: High-dimensional geometry confers computational benefits to DNN models of visual cortex. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion remains the same in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Representational similarity analysis demonstrated shared representations between ANN and BNN, particularly in the intraparietal sulcus.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Representational similarity analysis demonstrated shared representations between ANN and BNN, particularly in the intraparietal sulcus.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim, so the claim is unchanged in content and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Feature-brain similarity analysis reconstructed a sparse representation of mathematical operations based on distributed ANN features.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Feature-brain similarity analysis reconstructed a sparse representation of mathematical operations based on distributed ANN features in each cortical voxel.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the detail "in each cortical voxel," which clarifies and slightly narrows the original claim without changing confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The reconstruction of mathematical operations was more efficient when using features from deeper ANN layers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Reconstruction of mathematical operations was more efficient when using features from deeper ANN layers.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with the same level of certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Latent ANN features allowed the decoding of novel operators not used during model training from brain activity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Latent ANN features allowed the decoding of novel operators not used during model training from brain activity.
alignments.rationale: The content is the same but the publication reports this with higher certainty (high vs. medium), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The current study provides novel insights into the neural code underlying mathematical thought.
alignments.pub_claim_text: ANN-based distributed representations can explain brain activity patterns of symbolic mathematical operations.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the broad conclusion into a more specific explanatory claim about ANN-based representations for symbolic mathematical operations, narrowing and clarifying the original high-level conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep neural networks tend to base their predictions on overall genetic relatedness rather than on the effects of particular markers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep neural networks tend to base their predictions on overall genetic relatedness rather than on the effects of particular markers.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this conclusion verbatim, preserving content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The same network, provided with only the locations of matches between markers for two individuals, is able to perform prediction to the same level of accuracy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The same network, provided with only the locations of matches between markers for two individuals, is able to perform prediction to the same level of accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The result appears unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Powerful nonlinear models such as deep neural networks do not seem to outperform classical linear methods in genomic prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Powerful nonlinear models, such as deep neural networks, do not tend to outperform classical linear methods in genomic prediction.
alignments.rationale: Wording is slightly adjusted but the substantive conclusion and confidence are the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Training basecallers on taxon-specific data resulted in a significant boost in consensus accuracy, mostly due to the reduction of errors in methylation motifs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Training basecallers on taxon-specific data results in a significant boost in consensus accuracy, mostly due to the reduction of errors in methylation motifs.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this result with the same emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A larger neural network was able to improve both read and consensus accuracy, but at a cost to speed.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A larger neural network is able to improve both read and consensus accuracy, but at a cost to speed.
alignments.rationale: The claim is carried forward into the publication with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Improving consensus sequences ('polishing') with Nanopolish somewhat negates the accuracy differences in basecallers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Improving consensus sequences ('polishing') with Nanopolish somewhat negates the accuracy differences in basecallers, but pre-polish accuracy does have an effect on post-polish accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the original point about polishing negating differences but adds the nuance that pre-polish accuracy still affects post-polish accuracy, clarifying the effect.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pre-polish accuracy does have an effect on post-polish accuracy, so basecaller choice is still relevant even when Nanopolish is used.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Improving consensus sequences ('polishing') with Nanopolish somewhat negates the accuracy differences in basecallers, but pre-polish accuracy does have an effect on post-polish accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The publication incorporates this preprint conclusion into a single consolidated result that explicitly states the retained effect of pre-polish accuracy.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Basecalling accuracy has seen significant improvements over the last 2 years.
alignments.rationale: This broader historical claim about recent improvements is not present in the preprint and appears as an added concluding statement in the publication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: If higher accuracy is required, users should consider producing a custom model using a larger neural network and/or training data from the same species.
alignments.rationale: This practical recommendation is new in the publication and does not have a direct counterpart in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Evolved synthetic images evoked higher firing rates than even the best natural images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A genetic algorithm can evolve rich synthetic images that maximize neuronal firing in the cortex.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the ability to evolve images that maximize firing but omits the explicit comparative claim that they evoked higher rates than the best natural images, which tempers and revises the original strong comparative result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A generative deep neural network interacted with a genetic algorithm to evolve stimuli that maximized the firing of neurons in alert macaque inferotemporal and primary visual cortex.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A genetic algorithm can evolve rich synthetic images that maximize neuronal firing in the cortex.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same methodological result in slightly more general wording ("the cortex" rather than specific areas), keeping the substantive contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Distance in image space from the evolved images predicted responses of neurons to novel images.
alignments.rationale: This specific predictive result about distance in image space does not appear in the publication's summarized claims and seems omitted from the final paper.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Neuronal selectivity in monkey inferotemporal cortex can be investigated using a generative deep neural network.
alignments.rationale: This higher-level conclusion about the investigatory potential of generative DNNs is framed as a publication-level conclusion not explicitly stated in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The results expand our conception of the dictionary of features encoded in the cortex.
alignments.rationale: This interpretive conclusion is new in the publication and expresses broader implications that were not explicit in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach used in this study can potentially reveal the internal representations of any system whose input can be captured by a generative model.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a generalization about the method's potential applicability beyond the original preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: OrgaQuant is a deep convolutional neural network implementation that can locate and quantify the size distribution of intestinal organoids in brightfield images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: OrgaQuant is a deep convolutional neural network that can locate and quantify the size distribution of human intestinal organoids in brightfield images.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies "human" intestinal organoids, narrowing the scope while preserving the core result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: OrgaQuant requires no parameter tweaking and can be fully automated to analyze thousands of images with no user intervention.
alignments.pub_claim_text: OrgaQuant requires no parameter tweaking and can be fully automated to analyze thousands of images with no user intervention.
alignments.rationale: This conclusion is carried forward unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A unique dataset of manually annotated intestinal organoid images was created to develop OrgaQuant.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A unique dataset of manually annotated human intestinal organoid images has been created to develop OrgaQuant.
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the dataset as human organoid images, narrowing the scope but keeping the contribution the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The dataset, trained model, and inference scripts for OrgaQuant have been made publicly available along with detailed usage instructions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The dataset, trained model, and inference scripts for OrgaQuant have been made publicly available.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms public availability but omits mention of detailed usage instructions in the claim text, a minor clarification of wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Earlier ERP activity was best explained by early CNN layers at all electrodes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Earlier (0-200 ms) ERP activity was best explained by early CNN layers at all electrodes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim but adds an explicit time window (0–200 ms), providing temporal precision (clarification and narrowing of scope).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Later neural activity was best explained by the later, conceptual layers of the CNN. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Activity in right occipito-temporal electrodes was best explained by the later, fully-connected layers of the CNN around 225 ms post-stimulus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core result but specifies electrode location and timing (~225 ms) and layer type (fully-connected), narrowing and clarifying the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep artificial neural networks trained to perform scene categorization traverse similar representational stages as the human brain. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sequential scene category representations built by a CNN map onto those built in the human brain as assessed by high-density, time-resolved event-related potentials (ERPs). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same mapping between CNN and human scene representations using ERPs, retaining the original conclusion and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Examining these networks will allow neuroscientists to better understand the transformations that enable invariant visual categorization. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The emergence of scene category representations develops through a dynamic interplay between early activity over occipital electrodes and later activity over temporal and frontal electrodes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint framed a general contribution of studying networks to understand invariant categorization; the publication instead articulates a specific mechanistic conclusion about the dynamics and electrode regions, revising and narrowing the original broader contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SpatialDDLS is a fast neural network-based algorithm for cell type deconvolution of spatial transcriptomics data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SpatialDDLS is a fast neural network-based algorithm for cell type deconvolution of spatial transcriptomics data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the contribution verbatim with the same framing and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SpatialDDLS leverages single-cell RNA sequencing data to simulate mixed transcriptional profiles for training. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SpatialDDLS leverages single-cell RNA sequencing data to simulate mixed transcriptional profiles with predefined cellular composition. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the detail that simulated profiles have predefined cellular composition, clarifying the simulation procedure without changing the claim's scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SpatialDDLS demonstrates accuracy and speed as an alternative to existing state-of-the-art spatial deconvolution methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SpatialDDLS demonstrates accuracy and speed compared to two state-of-the-art spatial deconvolution methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the performance claim but specifies comparison to two particular state-of-the-art methods, adding precision to the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Convolutional neural networks can predict the phenotype of unannotated sequences with good accuracy when large quantities of labeled samples are available for training. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on a hybrid method and small-data performance; it does not restate the preprint's general claim about CNN performance given large labeled datasets, so this claim is effectively omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of convolutional neural networks is mitigated with medium- or small-scale datasets, necessitating new data-efficient approaches. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method performs better than traditional convolutional neural networks when the amount of training data is small. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication addresses the small-data limitation by reporting that their method outperforms CNNs on small datasets, which reframes the limitation into an empirical result demonstrating the proposed solution (clarification).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A hybrid approach between convolutional neural networks and kernel methods significantly improves performance when the amount of training data is small. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A hybrid approach between convolutional neural networks and kernel methods significantly improves the modeling of biological sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the hybrid approach improves sequence modeling (same contribution), keeping the claim and confidence intact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model is simple to interpret, which is crucial for discovering predictive motifs in sequences. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model is simple to interpret, which is crucial for discovering predictive motifs in sequences. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the interpretability claim and its importance for motif discovery but reduces stated certainty (high -> medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ResNet-18 architecture with simple data augmentation trained in 10-fold cross-validation performs multiclass classification relatively well even with a small imbalanced dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Image augmentation was used to address the challenge of imbalanced data distribution in the glioma dataset. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the use of data augmentation but frames it as a method to address imbalance; model and evaluation details changed in the paper, so the augmentation aspect is clarified while other specifics are altered elsewhere.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The protocol designed for deriving new biomarkers based on multiplex stained histological samples proved the ability to detect features characteristic of malignant tumors based only on single target stained tissue microarrays. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report this specific protocol/result about multiplex-to-single-target TMA biomarker derivation, so the claim is not present in the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis suggested a relevant role of the myeloid cells and their accumulation to characterize glioma grades. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tumor microenvironment analysis suggested an important role for myeloid cells and their accumulation in the context of characterizing glioma grades. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports essentially the same conclusion regarding myeloid cells and their relevance to glioma grading with similar confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This promising approach can be used as an additional diagnostic tool to improve assessment during intra-operative examination or sub-typing tissues for treatment selection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep learning approach for automatic multiclass classification of tumor grades was proposed to avoid manual examination of images. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The preprint envisioned a diagnostic adjunct; the publication frames a concrete contribution (automatic multiclass classification to avoid manual exam) with stronger emphasis and higher stated certainty, thus clarifying and somewhat amplifying the practical aim while narrowing focus to automating classification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further studies on this topic can help in formulating specific criteria for improvements in diagnosis of gliomas, allowing to avoid the necessity of conducting advanced histopathological analysis or complementing genetic testing of tumor samples. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The final paper does not include this speculative recommendation about avoiding advanced histopathology/genetic testing, so this forward-looking low-certainty claim was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models can learn the relationship between unaligned amino acid sequences and their functional annotations across all 17929 families of the Pfam database. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models can accurately predict functional annotations for unaligned amino acid sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core ability to predict function from unaligned sequences but drops the explicit claim about covering all 17,929 Pfam families, making the original broader statement more conservative (revised and narrowed).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Convolutional networks developed in this study are significantly more accurate and computationally efficient than BLASTp. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining deep models with existing methods significantly improves remote homology detection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's direct superiority claim over BLASTp is softened in the publication to a statement about combining deep models with existing methods to improve detection, which revises and narrows the nature of the performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model co-locates sequences from unseen families in embedding space, allowing for accurate annotation of sequences from novel families. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models predict function for 360 human reference proteome proteins with no previous Pfam annotation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication demonstrates a concrete instance (360 human proteins without Pfam annotation) of the preprint's embedding-based annotation capability, providing an applied example and narrowing the general claim to specific evidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results suggest that deep learning models will be a core component of future protein function prediction tools. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models will be a core component of future protein annotation tools. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same forward-looking conclusion but states it with higher certainty (low -> medium), amplifying the confidence in the original forecast.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepType significantly outperformed existing methods in identifying more robust cancer subtypes while using fewer genes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepType significantly outperformed existing methods in identifying more robust cancer subtypes while using fewer genes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication claim text are effectively identical in content and certainty, so no substantive change is observed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach provides a framework for the derivation of more accurate and robust molecular cancer subtypes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach provides a framework for the derivation of more accurate and robust molecular cancer subtypes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is carried forward verbatim from preprint to publication with no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepType leverages deep learning to disentangle and eliminate irrelevant factors in cancer subtype classification. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepType leverages deep learning to disentangle and eliminate irrelevant factors in cancer subtype classification. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The methodological/contribution claim is preserved exactly between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GARFIELD-NGS is a new tool that uses deep learning models to distinguish between false and true variants in exome sequencing experiments. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims emphasize performance and integration but do not explicitly restate that GARFIELD-NGS uses deep learning models to distinguish true/false variants, so the explicit contribution claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GARFIELD-NGS demonstrated strong performance for both SNP and INDEL variants with an AUC ranging from 0.71 to 0.98. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GARFIELD-NGS demonstrates strong performances for both SNP and INDEL variants with an AUC of 0.71-0.98. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric performance claim (AUC range) and wording are essentially identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GARFIELD-NGS outperformed established hard filters in variant detection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GARFIELD-NGS outperforms established hard filters in identifying true genetic variants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims assert superiority to hard filters for identifying true variants; wording differs slightly but meaning is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method is robust at low coverage down to 30X and is compatible with data generated using recent Illumina two-colour chemistry. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method is robust at low coverage down to 30X. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the low-coverage robustness claim but omits the compatibility with recent Illumina two-colour chemistry, narrowing the scope and tempering the original combined claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ISPRED-SEQ is a new method for predicting putative protein-protein interaction sites using protein sequences as input. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ISPRED-SEQ utilizes protein language models and Deep Neural networks for its predictions. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the modeling approach (protein language models and DNNs), which clarifies the preprint's method-that uses sequence input—without changing the contribution's substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ISPRED-SEQ outperforms state-of-the-art predictors in the prediction of protein-protein interactions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ISPRED-SEQ overpasses state-of-the-art predictors in predicting putative protein-protein interaction sites. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance claim is preserved between preprint and publication with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ISPRED-SEQ is freely available for testing online. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include availability; the preprint's explicit availability statement is not reflected among the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Random neural networks with a distribution of self-couplings generate multiple timescales of activity spanning several orders of magnitude. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neural circuits with heterogeneous neural assemblies of different sizes naturally generate multiple timescales of activity spanning several orders of magnitude. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core result (multiple timescales) is preserved but the mechanistic description shifts from heterogeneous self-couplings in random networks to heterogeneous assemblies of different sizes, a substantive revision of the proposed mechanism.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Slow and fast neural clusters preferentially entrain slow and fast spectral components of a time-dependent broadband input, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Large and small assemblies preferentially entrain slow and fast spectral components of the input, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the entrainment result but rephrases clusters as assemblies and input description is slightly condensed; the scientific claim remains the same with a revised framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings suggest a potential mechanism for spectral demixing in cortical circuits. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heterogeneous assemblies can provide a biologically plausible mechanism for neural circuits to demix complex temporal input signals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the mechanism as biologically plausible and broadens the target from spectral demixing to demixing of complex temporal inputs, increasing confidence and generality.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The alternative models trained using datasets derived from standing variation demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy, outperforming the widely used pathogenicity score CADD v1.6 in certain genomic regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The alternative models trained on standing variation datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy, comparable to deleteriousness scores of CADD v1.6 and v1.7. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication tempers the original claim of outperforming CADD v1.6 in certain regions to a statement of comparability to CADD v1.6 and v1.7, revising the strength of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Datasets derived from standing variation better represent allelic changes in the human genome and do not require extensive simulations and adaptations to annotations of the evolutionary-derived sequence alterations used for CADD training. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Datasets derived from standing variation represent natural allelic changes in the human genome without the need for extensive simulations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core conclusion that standing variation represents natural allelic changes and reduces emphasis on simulation needs; the substantive point is carried forward.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The training set created from frequent and rare variants in gnomAD 3.0 exceeds existing datasets in size and allows for genome-wide coverage of molecular effects. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed datasets derived from standing variation are substantially larger and potentially less biased than conventional datasets. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes larger size and potential reduced bias, which clarifies and extends the preprint's statement about size and coverage without changing the contribution's intent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The frequencies of pathogenic variants are decreasing, while beneficial alleles are increasing and frequencies of neutral variants are subject to drift. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include this population-genetics trend statement, so the preprint's specific claim about allele frequency trends was not carried into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating genotype-by-environment interaction effects into statistical models improved predictive ability by up to 0.08 for nine traits compared to the benchmark model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating genotype-by-environment interaction effects into statistical models improved predictive ability by up to 0.08 for nine traits compared to the benchmark model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication texts are effectively identical in wording and reported effect size, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning approach achieved the highest predictive ability for three traits with simpler genetic architectures, outperforming the benchmark by up to 0.10. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning approach achieved the highest predictive ability for three traits with oligogenic genetic architectures, outperforming the benchmark by up to 0.10. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication replaces the vague phrase 'simpler' with the more specific 'oligogenic', clarifying the genetic-architecture description while keeping effect size and claim scope the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Including non-additive effects slightly improved predictive ability by up to 0.03 for two traits, but enviromic-based effects resulted in no improvement. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not report the non-additive or enviromic-effect results present in the preprint, indicating this specific result was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tested statistical models capture genotype-by-environment interactions particularly well. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tested statistical models capture genotype-by-environment interactions particularly well. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is verbatim between preprint and publication, so it remains unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study will foster the adoption of multi-environmental genomic prediction to select apple cultivars adapted to diverse environmental conditions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study will foster the adoption of multi-environmental genomic prediction to select apple cultivars adapted to diverse environmental conditions. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution/forward-looking claim appears verbatim in the publication with the same level of certainty and intent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method is efficient in finding HIV-1 outbreaks with R 0 ≥ 3, achieving a specificity exceeding 85% and sensitivity better than 70%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method is efficient in finding HIV-1 outbreaks with R0 ≥ 2.5, achieving a specificity exceeding 98% and sensitivity better than 92%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication changes key numeric thresholds and reports substantially higher specificity/sensitivity, revising the quantitative claim; lowering the R0 cutoff broadens applicability and the performance figures amplify confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We accurately identified known outbreak sequences in the background of slower spreading HIV. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model accurately identified known outbreak sequences in the background of slower spreading HIV. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The identification claim is consistent in both versions with no substantive change in wording or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our approach is scalable to processing hundreds of thousands of sequences, making it useful for real-time epidemiological investigations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach is scalable to processing hundreds of thousands of sequences, making it useful for current and future real-time epidemiological investigations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the scalability claim but slightly extends its framing to 'current and future' investigations, which modestly amplifies the implication of usefulness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We detected both outbreaks early on, implying that this method could have enabled timely intervention to prevent the extent of these outbreaks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We detected both outbreaks early on, implying that had this method been applied in real-time, one could have intervened to possibly prevent the extent of these outbreaks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same counterfactual conclusion with slightly more explicit conditional phrasing, clarifying the hypothetical intervention scenario.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed deep learning system achieves a 93% patient-level accuracy in predicting pathological complete response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in triple-negative breast cancer patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed deep learning system achieves a 93% patient-level accuracy in predicting pathological complete response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in triple-negative breast cancer patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is identically reported in the publication with the same accuracy figure and context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning model outperforms baselines and other state-of-the-art systems in predicting pathological complete response. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model outperforms baselines and other state-of-the-art systems in predicting pathological complete response. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The comparative performance claim is preserved verbatim between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The introduction of a human knowledge derived spatial attention mechanism enhances the interpretability of the deep learning system. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The introduction of a human knowledge-derived spatial attention mechanism enhances the interpretability of the deep learning model. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution about a human-knowledge-derived attention mechanism and its interpretability benefit appears in both versions with equivalent wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pathological complete response is regarded as a strong predictor of overall survival in triple-negative breast cancer patients. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This broader clinical interpretation about pCR predicting overall survival appears in the preprint but is not present among the publication claims provided.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Seventy-three of the 1955 procured molecules bound LRRK2 in an SPR assay with K D lower than 150 μM. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Of the 1955 molecules predicted by participants in Round 1 of the challenge, 73 were found to bind to LRRK2 in an SPR assay with a K<sub>D</sub> lower than 150 μM. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric screening result and assay details are the same in both versions, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Binding was observed in two orthogonal assays with affinities ranging from 18 to 140 μM for seven chemically diverse series. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Binding was observed in two orthogonal assays for seven chemically diverse series, with affinities ranging from 18 to 140 μM. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The binding observation across assays and the reported affinity range are equivalently described in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine-learning accelerated methods achieved similar results to brute force docking. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning-accelerated methods achieved similar results to brute force docking. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The comparative conclusion about method performance is carried over verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: First-in-class, experimentally confirmed compounds were rare and weakly potent. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: First-in-class, experimentally confirmed compounds were rare and weakly potent, indicating that recent advances are not sufficient to effectively address challenging targets. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the original observation but adds an interpretive implication about the sufficiency of recent advances, clarifying and broadening the conclusion and strengthening its assessed significance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ensemble of three deep learning models was developed to predict outcomes in the BVP assay and the BSA binding assay. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims discuss model performance and PLM details but do not explicitly restate the development-of-an-ensemble claim, suggesting this specific formulation was not carried into the publication's highlighted claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The resulting models demonstrated robust performance on normal mAbs, bispecific Abs, and single-domain Fc (VHH-Fc). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The resulting models demonstrated robust performance on canonical mAbs, bispecific Abs, and single-domain Fc (VHH-Fc). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication uses 'canonical mAbs' instead of 'normal mAbs' but otherwise repeats the performance claim; wording is slightly clarified while meaning is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Protein language models outperformed a model built using molecular descriptors calculated from AlphaFold 2 predicted structures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PLMs outperformed a model built using molecular descriptors calculated from AlphaFold 2 predicted structures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core comparative performance claim between PLMs and descriptor-based models is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This represents the first application of protein language models to predict assay data on bispecifics and VHH-Fcs. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This represents the first application of PLMs to predict assay data on bispecifics and VHH-Fcs. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The novelty claim about first application of PLMs to these molecule classes is retained with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A recently proposed deep learning-based virtual screening model, PointVS, identified the most important functional groups with 39% more efficiency than a fingerprint-based random forest. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A recently proposed deep learning-based virtual screening model, PointVS, identified the most important functional groups with 39% more efficiency than a fingerprint-based random forest. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text is effectively identical between preprint and publication; claim content and emphasis are unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ligand-specific biases present in widely used virtual screening datasets substantially impaired the ability of all machine learning models to identify the most important functional groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ligand-specific biases present in widely used virtual screening datasets substantially impaired the ability of all machine learning models to identify the most important functional groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is phrased the same in both versions; no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed protocol for generating synthetic data allows for precise quantification of the ground truth importance of each atom in protein-ligand complexes. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed protocol for generating synthetic data allows for precise quantification of the ground truth importance of each atom in protein-ligand complexes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Textual claim is the same but the publication expresses higher certainty, so content unchanged while confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The synthetic data generation framework has been made available to facilitate the benchmarking of new virtual screening models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The synthetic data generation framework has been made available to facilitate the benchmarking of new virtual screening models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Identical contribution claim; no substantive change between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Orbit Image Analysis is an open-source tool designed for whole slide image analysis using a tile-based map-reduce execution framework. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Orbit Image Analysis is an open-source tool designed for whole slide image analysis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication omits the explicit 'tile-based map-reduce execution framework' detail present in the preprint, reducing specificity of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tool integrates a deep learning segmentation method for complex object detection. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Orbit has machine-learning capabilities that enable deep learning segmentation and complex object detection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions state deep learning segmentation and complex object detection capabilities; wording differs but meaning is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Orbit demonstrates effective application in quantifying idiopathic lung fibrosis, nerve fibre density, and glomeruli detection in kidney. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Orbit has been applied in real-world applications including quantification of idiopathic lung fibrosis, nerve fibre density quantification, and glomeruli detection in the kidney. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication lists the same application examples and maintains the same result claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The system allows for the processing of large images by dividing them into smaller parts for analysis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tool utilizes the Apache Spark framework for distributed computing, enhancing its scale-out functionality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's general statement about splitting large images is made more concrete in the publication by specifying Apache Spark distributed computing; the core capability is consistent but more specific.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Orbit can operate as a standalone application or connect to the open-source image server OMERO. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This capability (OMERO connectivity/standalone operation) appears in the publication but is not stated among the preprint claims, so it is an added publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Brain network patterns reliably classified chronic pain patients with an accuracy of 63% in an independent data set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning and deep learning allowed reliable classification of patients in a third, independent open data set with an accuracy of 63%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same classification accuracy on an independent dataset; content and confidence are consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning classifier using a conditional variational autoencoder also yielded 63% generalisation accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning and deep learning allowed reliable classification of patients in a third, independent open data set with an accuracy of 63%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's statement about both machine learning and deep learning classification encompasses this specific deep learning classifier result from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is consistent evidence of hub disruption in the brain network of chronic pain patients based on clustering and betweenness centrality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified robust evidence of network hub disruption in chronic pain, most consistently with respect to clustering coefficient and betweenness centrality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the same hub disruption finding with the same metrics; content is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant changes in the modular identity of several brain regions were revealed, particularly in the bilateral sensorimotor cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found a consensus pattern of modular reorganisation involving extensive, bilateral regions of sensorimotor cortex, characterised primarily by negative reorganisation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same modular reorganisation in sensorimotor cortex; phrasing differs but the substantive result is consistent.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The results provide evidence of consistent and characteristic brain network changes in chronic pain, characterised primarily by extensive reorganisation of the network architecture of the sensorimotor cortex. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This broader concluding synthesis appears in the published version but is not listed among the discrete preprint claims; it summarizes the combined findings.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PoxiPred is an AI-based tool developed to predict potential antigens and T-cell epitopes for multiple poxviruses. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PoxiPred is an AI-based tool developed to predict potential antigens and T-cell epitopes for multiple poxviruses. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Contribution claim matches exactly between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PoxiPred was able to locate 1,675 antigen proteins from 25 distinct poxviruses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PoxiPred was able to locate 3191 antigen proteins from 25 distinct poxviruses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is the same in kind but the reported count of antigen proteins changes substantially in the publication, so the result is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: From the identified antigenic proteins, PoxiPred located 6,579 T-cell epitopes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: From the identified antigenic proteins, PoxiPred redundantly located up to five epitopes per protein, resulting in 16,817 potential T-cell epitopes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication reports a larger, more detailed epitope count and notes redundancy per protein; the basic result is updated and broadened in scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PoxiPred can identify antigens and T-cell epitopes for poxviruses with a single input, the proteome file of any poxvirus. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The operational-convenience claim (single-input proteome-based identification) is not present among the publication claims and appears to have been omitted.

alignments.pub_claim_text: 92% of the predicted T-cell epitopes were identified as being reactive to CD8+ T-cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes this additional validation/result that was not listed among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Integrating crosslinking mass spectrometry data substantially improves modelling performance on challenging targets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Integrating crosslinking mass spectrometry data substantially improves modelling performance on challenging targets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core empirical claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integration of experimental distance restraints helps to identify interfaces, focus sampling, and improve model selection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integration of experimental distance restraints helps to identify interfaces and focuses sampling in protein complex modelling. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains identification of interfaces and focused sampling but omits the explicit 'improve model selection' phrase from the preprint, narrowing the asserted benefits.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This approach extends to single crosslinks from whole-cell crosslinking mass spectrometry, enabling whole-cell structural investigations driven by experimental data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach opens the possibility of whole-cell structural investigations driven by experimental data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication tones the claim from an asserted extension to 'opens the possibility', which clarifies and tempers the strength of the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We reveal the molecular basis of iron homeostasis in Bacillus subtilis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We reveal the molecular basis of iron homoeostasis in Bacillus subtilis through this method. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result claim is retained in the publication (spelling variant only); content and confidence remain the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GeneSegNet significantly improves cell segmentation performances over existing methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GeneSegNet significantly improves cell segmentation performances over existing methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication claim are textually and semantically identical about improved segmentation performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GeneSegNet integrates both gene expression and imaging information to perform cell segmentation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GeneSegNet integrates both gene expression and imaging information to perform cell segmentation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same integration of gene expression and imaging for segmentation without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GeneSegNet employs a recursive training strategy to deal with noisy training labels. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GeneSegNet employs a recursive training strategy to deal with noisy training labels. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The recursive training strategy claim is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepArk accurately predicts the presence of thousands of different context-specific regulatory features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepArk accurately predicts the presence of thousands of different context-specific regulatory features, including chromatin states, histone marks, and transcription factors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds concrete examples of the regulatory features predicted (chromatin states, histone marks, TFs), clarifying but not changing the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepArk can predict the regulatory impact of any genomic variant, including rare or not previously observed variants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In vivo studies show that DeepArk can predict the regulatory impact of any genomic variant, including rare or not previously observed variants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that the prediction claim is supported by in vivo studies, which strengthens (amplifies) confidence while retaining the original scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepArk enables the regulatory annotation of understudied model species. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepArk enables the regulatory annotation of understudied model species. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This conclusion is carried forward verbatim from preprint to publication with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The electronic nose was able to differentiate insect species with an accuracy of 89.64%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The electronic nose was able to differentiate insect species up to gender level with an accuracy of 89.64%. (certainty=high, type=result)
